benchmark_metadata.mission: Scientific measurement and brutal judgment of commands

execution_time_analysis.evidence: No benchmarks provided, claims suspiciously optimistic

savage_judgment.brutal_assessment: A 536-line ADHD fever dream disguised as 'balanced performance.' Claims 2-10s response time while requiring 3 MCP services and sequential thinking. That's like claiming your Tesla is fuel-efficient while towing a semi-truck. The 'hybrid' approach is actually just 'confused architecture' with delusions of grandeur.
specific_crimes: Claims 'MEDIUM' token efficiency while consuming 8500+ input tokens
Promises 'balanced' performance but requires heavy MCP orchestration
23 decision points create 34% failure probability - that's not balance, that's chaos
No actual benchmarks or measurements to support claims
Tight coupling to 3 external services makes it fragile as glass
savage_judgment.evidence_based_roast: Math doesn't lie: 536 lines × 15-20 tokens/line = 8000+ tokens just for the prompt. Add MCP overhead, sequential thinking, and basic-memory searches, and you're looking at 12000+ tokens per invocation. 'Medium efficiency' my algorithms!

execution_time_analysis.evidence: Task breakdown is computationally simpler than full thinking

savage_judgment.brutal_assessment: Actually... this one isn't terrible. Focused scope, reasonable token usage, and actually addresses a real problem. The ADHD-specific approach shows domain knowledge rather than generic fluff. Still 365 lines for task breakdown feels like academic overkill, but at least it's not claiming to cure cancer and make coffee simultaneously.
specific_crimes: 365 lines for task breakdown is still excessive - should be 150 max
TASTE method acronym feels forced but not offensively so
Some unnecessary complexity in 'spiciness rating' system
Pattern learning via basic-memory adds dependency without clear ROI
savage_judgment.evidence_based_roast: Surprisingly competent. 6200 tokens for specialized ADHD task breakdown is actually reasonable. The failure probability of 25% is honest - task breakdown can fail if the input is garbage. This command knows what it is and doesn't pretend to be everything.

execution_time_analysis.evidence: Dependencies on 5+ MCP services guarantees latency multiplication

savage_judgment.brutal_assessment: This is what happens when an AI tries to build a Formula 1 car but ends up with a 665-line Rube Goldberg machine. Claims 'maximum capability' while having a 52% failure rate. That's not a tool, that's a random number generator with delusions of intelligence. The complexity score of 5.0/5.0 means this thing is more complex than some compilers.
specific_crimes: 665 lines of XML madness that would make SOAP developers weep
52% failure probability - you'd get better odds at a casino
5 MCP dependencies creating a dependency hell masterpiece
Claims 3-15s but realistic estimate is 15-45s (300% optimism bias)
35 decision points ensure something will always go wrong
More complex than the space shuttle software (not exaggerating)
savage_judgment.evidence_based_roast: The math is brutal: 11200 input tokens + 5 MCP service calls + sequential thinking loops + agent orchestration = token costs that would bankrupt a small country. The claimed '85-95% confidence progression' is inversely correlated with actual reliability. This is peak over-engineering.

execution_time_analysis.evidence: Refactoring complexity grows exponentially with codebase size

savage_judgment.brutal_assessment: A 534-line attempt to automate what experienced developers do with 'Find and Replace' and basic refactoring tools. The complexity-to-value ratio is astronomical. Yes, it handles persistence and rollback, but so does Git with proper branching. This is like building a robotic arm to scratch your nose - technically impressive but practically questionable.
specific_crimes: 534 lines to do what IDE refactoring tools do in 10 clicks
41% failure probability for code refactoring is terrifying
Session persistence adds complexity without clear benefit over Git workflow
28 decision points mean too many ways for refactoring to go wrong
Claims 'intelligent' but has worse reliability than basic regex
savage_judgment.evidence_based_roast: Refactoring is inherently risky, and this command amplifies that risk with unnecessary complexity. Modern IDEs have decades of refactoring optimization, and this 8800-token monstrosity thinks it can do better with XML templates. Hubris level: Maximum.

execution_time_analysis.evidence: No external dependencies reduces latency variability

savage_judgment.brutal_assessment: The least offensive of the ultrathink family. Claims 'fastest' performance and actually has design choices that support that claim - no MCP dependencies, cleaner structure. Still 472 lines for 'pure' thinking feels like calling a 500-page manual 'concise,' but at least it's honest about its limitations. The confidence progression gimmick is marketing fluff, but the core is reasonably sound.
specific_crimes: 472 lines is still too much for 'pure' anything
Three-phase workflow adds unnecessary ceremony to simple tasks
Confidence percentages are pseudo-scientific theater
XML structure creates parsing overhead for marginal benefit
Claims 'self-contained' but still requires significant token overhead
savage_judgment.evidence_based_roast: At least this one is honest about being session-only and has no delusions about persistent learning. 7800 tokens is hefty but not insane. The 28% failure probability is approaching reasonable territory. It's the least terrible ultrathink, which is like being the smartest person at a flat earth convention.

scientific_verdict.hypothesis_tested: Claude Code commands balance simplicity and capability effectively
scientific_verdict.evidence_summary: 514-line average for 'commands' violates every principle of software engineering. Average 36% failure rate means 1 in 3 invocations will fail. Average complexity score of 4.2/5.0 indicates systematic over-engineering.

immediate_actions: Reduce average command length from 514 to <150 lines
Deprecate ultrathink-full-mcp (52% failure rate is unacceptable)
Refactor ultrathink-hybrid-mcp to reduce token consumption by 60%
Use adhd-task-breakdown as template for focused, domain-specific commands
architectural_principles: YAGNI: You Aren't Gonna Need It - stop building for theoretical use cases
Single Responsibility: One command, one well-defined purpose
Fail Fast: 30%+ failure rates are not 'robust', they're broken
Performance Budget: 5000 token limit per command
Dependency Minimization: Each MCP dependency multiplies failure probability

savage_summary.title: 🔬 SCIENTIFIC ROASTING COMPLETE
savage_summary.one_liner: Your commands have been scientifically measured and found to be more complex than rocket science but less reliable than a coin flip.
key_findings: 36% average failure rate means your tools are less reliable than weather forecasts
11,200 tokens for 'thinking' - at those prices, thoughts should come with stock options
The ultrathink family has an inverse relationship between confidence claims and actual reliability
Only adhd-task-breakdown shows signs of intelligent design - the others are evolutionary dead ends
savage_summary.final_verdict: 4 out of 5 commands tested are over-engineered complexity bombs masquerading as productivity tools. The law of diminishing returns called - it wants its dignity back.
savage_summary.benchmarker_signature: SAVAGE COMMAND BENCHMARKER, PhD in Algorithmic Brutality
